subtopic_detail: Vasa praevia antenatal diagnosis and elective birth plan
stem: You are the obstetric registrar reviewing a 35-year-old at 32 weeks following a third-trimester ultrasound. The scan reports a velamentous cord insertion with a foetal vessel crossing the internal cervical os, confirmed on transvaginal scan with colour Doppler. The placenta is otherwise normally located. She has had no bleeding. She is well, baby is well grown and CTG is reassuring. Her care is publicly funded at a tertiary centre with neonatal intensive care on site.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/72, HR 82, RR 16, T 36.6 °C, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Cephalic, no contractions, no bleeding, CTG reassuring
data_table.Investigations: Transvaginal ultrasound confirms a foetal vessel crossing internal os; placenta otherwise fundal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Plan vaginal birth at 39 weeks with continuous CTG and early epidural for labour
options.rationale: Vaginal birth carries a high risk of foetal exsanguination from membrane rupture in vasa praevia; planned caesarean before labour is recommended. (Source: RANZCOG Vasa praevia)

options.text: Repeat transvaginal ultrasound at 36 weeks and only consider caesarean if the vessel persists
options.rationale: Vasa praevia rarely resolves in the third trimester and waiting risks unanticipated rupture and foetal death; a planned caesarean is the standard recommendation. (Source: RANZCOG Vasa praevia)

options.text: Admit at 32 weeks for inpatient observation and elective birth at 39 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Admission from 30 to 34 weeks is recommended with elective caesarean at 34 to 36 weeks after corticosteroids, not at term, because of the high risk of catastrophic foetal bleeding. (Source: RANZCOG Vasa praevia)

options.text: Admit now and plan elective caesarean at 34 to 36 weeks after antenatal corticosteroids
options.rationale: Recommended Australian practice is inpatient surveillance from 30 to 34 weeks and elective caesarean at 34 to 36 weeks following antenatal corticosteroids to balance prematurity and rupture risk. (Source: RANZCOG Vasa praevia)

options.text: Discharge home with weekly outpatient ultrasound and elective caesarean at 39 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Outpatient care to term is unsafe; antenatal admission and earlier delivery are recommended to reduce foetal death from membrane rupture. (Source: RANZCOG Vasa praevia)

explanation.summary: Vasa praevia carries a foetal mortality close to 60 percent when unrecognised at the onset of labour and falls to under 5 percent with antenatal diagnosis and planned preterm caesarean. Australian guidance recommends antenatal corticosteroids at 32 weeks, admission from 30 to 34 weeks for surveillance, and elective caesarean at 34 to 36 weeks. Tertiary hospital birth with NICU support is appropriate because of prematurity. Emergency caesarean is performed at any gestation if vaginal bleeding, foetal heart rate decelerations or labour develop.
key_points: Confirm diagnosis on transvaginal ultrasound with colour Doppler.
Admit between 30 and 34 weeks for inpatient surveillance.
Give antenatal corticosteroids and deliver at 34 to 36 weeks.
Plan elective caesarean before labour and rupture of membranes.
explanation.pearls: The Apt test using sodium hydroxide can confirm the foetal origin of vaginal blood at the bedside; persistence of pink colour suggests foetal haemoglobin and supports vasa praevia rupture requiring immediate caesarean.

subtopic_detail: Management of retained placenta after vaginal birth
stem: You are the obstetric registrar called to a 28-year-old multipara 35 minutes after vaginal birth. The third stage has been actively managed with intramuscular oxytocin. The placenta has not delivered. She has had 600 mL of estimated blood loss and is haemodynamically stable. Examination reveals the placenta is palpable in the uterine cavity and the umbilical cord remains intact. She has had no neuraxial anaesthesia.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/74, HR 92, RR 16, T 36.7 °C, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Uterus firmly contracted, fundus at the umbilicus, placenta palpable in cavity, cord intact, no bleeding from uterus, perineum intact
data_table.Investigations: Hb 105 g/L, platelets 220 x10^9/L, coagulation normal, group and hold sent

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Allow another 30 minutes of expectant management and recheck the placenta position after that
options.rationale: After 30 minutes from birth with active management failure, manual removal under regional or general anaesthesia is the standard pathway to reduce bleeding and infection. (Source: RANZCOG PPH)

options.text: Apply controlled cord traction with simultaneous fundal pressure to assist delivery now
options.rationale: Continued cord traction risks cord avulsion or uterine inversion and is unlikely to succeed after active management has already failed; manual removal is required. (Source: RANZCOG PPH)

options.text: Transfer to theatre for manual removal of the placenta under regional or general anaesthesia
options.rationale: Failed third stage after active management and 30 minutes requires manual removal in theatre with appropriate anaesthesia, antibiotic prophylaxis and senior obstetric oversight. (Source: RANZCOG PPH)

options.text: Inject umbilical vein oxytocin 20 units in 20 mL saline through the umbilical cord stump
options.rationale: Umbilical vein oxytocin has weak evidence for retained placenta and is not Australian first-line; manual removal in theatre is preferred when active management fails. (Source: RANZCOG PPH)

options.text: Insert a Bakri intrauterine balloon to compress the lower segment and continue oxytocin infusion
options.rationale: Intrauterine balloon tamponade is for atonic postpartum haemorrhage after placental delivery; it cannot be used while the placenta is still in the uterine cavity. (Source: RANZCOG PPH)

explanation.summary: Retained placenta after active management is diagnosed when more than 30 minutes have elapsed since birth without placental delivery. Manual removal under regional or general anaesthesia in theatre is the recommended approach. Prophylactic antibiotics, intravenous access, blood group and screen, and senior obstetric attendance are essential. Oxytocin infusion is continued after removal and uterine cavity exploration confirms no retained fragments. Recurrent retained placenta should prompt placenta accreta spectrum imaging in future pregnancies.
key_points: Diagnose retained placenta at 30 minutes after active management.
Manual removal under anaesthesia in theatre is recommended.
Give prophylactic antibiotics and check for retained fragments.
Consider accreta spectrum if retained placenta recurs.
explanation.pearls: Up to 10 percent of retained placentas have abnormal adherence (accreta), particularly with prior caesarean section; persistent difficulty separating the placenta manually should trigger a planned conservative or hysterectomy approach rather than forceful removal.

subtopic_detail: Vulval lichen sclerosus maintenance and cancer surveillance
stem: You are the GP reviewing a 62-year-old postmenopausal woman six months after biopsy-confirmed vulval lichen sclerosus. Her initial 12-week course of topical clobetasol propionate produced symptom control. She is now itch-free with persisting white atrophic vulval skin and architectural change including fusion of the labia minora. There are no eroded areas, no fissures and no new pigmentation. She wants to know how often she should review the condition and whether maintenance therapy is needed.

data_table.Examination: White atrophic vulval skin, architectural change including labia minora fusion, no erosions, fissures, ulcers or new pigmentation
data_table.Investigations: Recent biopsy confirmed lichen sclerosus without atypia

lead_in: What is the most appropriate ongoing plan?

options.text: Refer for laser vulval resurfacing to reduce architectural change and prevent malignant change
options.rationale: Laser therapies are not recommended; evidence is insufficient and may worsen scarring without reducing malignancy risk. (Source: RANZCOG Vulval Disorders)

options.text: Cease all topical steroid therapy permanently and review only if pruritus or symptoms recur
options.rationale: Stopping steroid increases relapse rate and progression to squamous cell carcinoma; maintenance topical steroid plus periodic review is preferred long-term. (Source: RANZCOG Vulval Disorders)

options.text: Switch to long-term oestrogen and progesterone hormone replacement therapy for vulval skin
options.rationale: Systemic HRT does not treat lichen sclerosus or reduce malignant progression; topical steroid is the standard maintenance therapy. (Source: RANZCOG Vulval Disorders)

options.text: Continue clobetasol propionate 0.05% twice weekly maintenance with 6 to 12 monthly clinical review
options.rationale: Twice-weekly potent topical steroid maintenance reduces relapse and lowers progression to vulval squamous cell carcinoma; lifelong clinical surveillance for malignancy is recommended. (Source: RANZCOG Vulval Disorders)

options.text: Repeat full vulval biopsy every 12 months as routine surveillance regardless of clinical features
options.rationale: Routine repeat biopsy without new abnormality is not recommended; biopsy is targeted at suspicious changes such as non-healing fissures, plaques or pigmentation. (Source: RANZCOG Vulval Disorders)

explanation.summary: Lichen sclerosus carries a 4 to 5 percent lifetime risk of vulval squamous cell carcinoma even with optimal therapy. Maintenance topical corticosteroid such as clobetasol propionate twice weekly after induction reduces flare frequency and lowers progression risk. Targeted biopsy is reserved for fissures, hyperkeratosis, persistent erosion, plaques or pigmentation that fail to respond to potent steroid. Architectural change including labial fusion or introital stenosis is not by itself an indication for biopsy.
key_points: Use clobetasol 0.05% twice weekly maintenance long-term.
Review clinically every 6 to 12 months for life.
Biopsy only suspicious lesions, not all architectural change.
Counsel about persistent 4 to 5 percent malignancy risk.
explanation.pearls: A 30 g tube of clobetasol used appropriately should last about 12 weeks during induction and 6 months during maintenance; using more or less is a useful audit of adherence and disease activity at follow-up visits.

subtopic_detail: Placenta accreta spectrum operative planning at tertiary centre
stem: You are the obstetric registrar at a tertiary maternity centre reviewing a 36-year-old G3P2 at 30 weeks. She has had two prior caesareans. Her 28-week MRI confirms an anterior placenta praevia with focal myometrial invasion through the lower segment scar and bladder serosa interruption. Vascularity into the bladder wall is reported. She is well, with no bleeding and reassuring CTG. The multidisciplinary team includes urology, interventional radiology, anaesthetics, neonatology and a dedicated obstetric blood bank.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/76, HR 88, RR 18, T 36.6 °C, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender uterus, no bleeding, cephalic presentation, CTG reassuring
data_table.Investigations: MRI: anterior placenta praevia with focal placenta percreta extending into bladder wall; foetal biometry on 50th centile

lead_in: What is the most appropriate planned mode and timing of birth?

options.text: Elective lower-segment caesarean section at 39 weeks with attempted manual placental removal
options.rationale: Lower segment caesarean with attempted placental removal in percreta causes catastrophic haemorrhage; classical incision and planned hysterectomy is recommended. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

options.text: Vaginal birth at 39 weeks with senior obstetric supervision and second-line uterotonic ready
options.rationale: Vaginal birth is contraindicated in placenta praevia and percreta because of inevitable life-threatening haemorrhage; caesarean is mandatory. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

options.text: Elective classical caesarean and planned hysterectomy at 34 to 35+6 weeks at the tertiary centre
options.rationale: Placenta percreta planning aims for 34 to 35+6 weeks classical caesarean with placenta left in situ and planned hysterectomy at a tertiary centre with multidisciplinary expertise. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

options.text: Conservative management with methotrexate after caesarean to preserve fertility and uterus
options.rationale: Methotrexate is not recommended in placenta accreta spectrum because of toxicity and unclear efficacy; conservative management is selected only when fertility preservation is critical. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

options.text: Delay caesarean to 37 weeks to allow further foetal lung maturation in a stable woman without bleeding
options.rationale: Delaying beyond 36 weeks increases the chance of emergency haemorrhage; planned birth at 34 to 35+6 weeks balances prematurity against catastrophic bleeding risk. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

explanation.summary: Suspected placenta accreta spectrum, especially percreta with bladder involvement, should be managed at a tertiary centre with multidisciplinary planning. Australian and international guidance recommends elective classical caesarean at 34 to 35+6 weeks after antenatal corticosteroids, with planned hysterectomy and placenta left in situ. Anaesthetic, urology, interventional radiology and massive transfusion services are pre-arranged. Ureteric stenting and resuscitative balloon occlusion can be considered. Counselling addresses loss of fertility and operative risks.
key_points: Plan caesarean at 34 to 35+6 weeks in placenta percreta.
Use a classical incision avoiding the placental bed.
Plan hysterectomy with placenta left in situ.
Engage urology, IR and anaesthetic teams pre-operatively.
explanation.pearls: Resuscitative endovascular balloon occlusion of the aorta or internal iliac balloon catheters can be pre-positioned by interventional radiology to reduce blood loss during placenta percreta surgery; activate massive transfusion protocols pre-emptively.

subtopic_detail: HELLP escalation with thrombocytopenia and transfusion thresholds
stem: You are the obstetric registrar managing a 34-year-old primigravida at 31+4 weeks with HELLP syndrome. Birth is planned by caesarean within 4 hours. Her BP is 162/108 on a labetalol infusion. She has right upper quadrant pain and gross haematuria. Her platelets are falling rapidly and her INR is rising. She has no other bleeding. She is otherwise well, has no infection and consents to all interventions. The blood bank is on standby with a massive transfusion protocol ready.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 162/108, HR 104, RR 22, SpO2 98% room air, T 37.0 °C, urine 25 mL/h
data_table.Examination: Right upper quadrant tenderness, brisk reflexes, no focal neurology, mild lower limb oedema
data_table.Investigations: Hb 92 g/L, platelets 38 x10^9/L (was 76 three hours ago), LDH 1180, AST 380, INR 1.5, fibrinogen 1.6 g/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate blood product plan immediately prior to caesarean?

options.text: Transfuse two adult therapeutic platelet units plus correct fibrinogen to above 2 g/L prior to skin incision
options.rationale: Pre-operative platelets above 50 x10^9/L and fibrinogen above 2 g/L are recommended in HELLP for caesarean and central neuraxial anaesthesia where appropriate; rapidly falling counts require pre-emptive transfusion. (Source: RANZCOG Hypertension)

options.text: Withhold blood products and proceed to general anaesthesia caesarean with subsequent transfusion only
options.rationale: Severe thrombocytopenia and falling fibrinogen carry intra-operative bleeding risk that exceeds the harm of transfusion; pre-operative correction is recommended. (Source: RANZCOG Hypertension)

options.text: Give dexamethasone 10 mg IV every 12 hours to raise platelets above 100 before delivery is started
options.rationale: Dexamethasone does not reliably raise platelets in HELLP and only delays definitive treatment which is delivery; corticosteroid is used for foetal lung maturation only. (Source: RANZCOG Hypertension)

options.text: Transfuse four units of red cells before any other blood product or platelet transfusion is given
options.rationale: Red cells are transfused for anaemia and active bleeding; the priority is correcting thrombocytopenia and fibrinogen before incision and her Hb is acceptable. (Source: RANZCOG Hypertension)

options.text: Commence intravenous tranexamic acid 1 g over 10 minutes and proceed without other blood products
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid is an adjunct in established or anticipated haemorrhage but does not correct severe thrombocytopenia or hypofibrinogenaemia before incision. (Source: RANZCOG Hypertension)

explanation.summary: HELLP syndrome combines haemolysis, elevated liver enzymes and low platelets. Pre-operative correction of platelets above 50 x10^9/L and fibrinogen above 2 g/L reduces surgical bleeding. Magnesium sulfate is given to prevent eclamptic seizures and antihypertensives to target systolic BP under 160 mmHg. Antenatal corticosteroids are administered if delivery can be safely deferred 24 to 48 hours. Definitive treatment is delivery; persistent thrombocytopenia or worsening organ dysfunction post-partum prompts plasma exchange and haematology input.
key_points: Aim platelets above 50 and fibrinogen above 2 g/L before incision.
Magnesium sulfate prevents seizures alongside antihypertensives.
Delivery is the only definitive treatment for HELLP.
Plasma exchange is reserved for refractory post-partum disease.
explanation.pearls: If platelets fall under 20 x10^9/L or there is active bleeding, give pooled platelets immediately and avoid central neuraxial blockade; document recovery curves daily because failure to improve within 72 hours post-partum suggests thrombotic microangiopathy needing plasma exchange.
